# From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory
[Image: From Debits to Dominance: Pink Team (cPINKa) Credits the Ultimate Gamble Victory]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, March 1, 2026**
Accountancy students of Gordon College gathered at East Tapinac Covered Court on February 25 for Junior Philippine Institute of Accountants (JPIA)’s Ultimate Gamble Team Building.
The participants collaborated, competed, and conquered through strategy-driven games that tested teamwork and unity.
Demy Anne M. Roallos headed the Green Team (Berde Makausad), Triskelly Lianne Cyril Cabildo led the Red Team (Pulang Ina), Felicity Dyle Rivas guided the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca), Aran Benz C. Sardañas captained the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada), Shakira Nicole B. Guintu steered the Pink Team (cPINKa – Certified Pink Accountants), Jan Lester M. Bitangcol led the Purple Team (Ooh-Lila), and Hannah Jhem M. Malit directed the Blue Team (BIR – Bluereau of Impeccable Recorders).
As the event began, each team performed their energetic yells and presented their flags to start the event with a blast. In the first game entitled “Caterpillar Game,” Green Team (Berde Makausad) won first place, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) gained second place, and Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) took third place.
For the second game of “Pass the Balloon,” Red Team (Pulang Ina) achieved the first place, followed by the Pink Team (cPINKa), and lastly, the Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca). As the third game commenced, entitled “The Floor is Lava,” Pink Team (cPINKa) was hailed as the champion, Green Team (Berde Makausad) as the first runner-up, and the Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) as the second runner-up.
Moreover, in the station games, the teams played in seven stations, namely Pass the Ball, Cut it Up, Wrap-tastic, Balloon Cup Relay, Rollercoaster Ball, T-Shirt Relay, and Calcu Relay. Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) triumphed in first place, Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) succeeded in second place, while Pink Team (cPINKa) acquired third place.
After consolidating all performance entries like preparing a final financial statement, Pink Team (cPINKa) emerged as the overall champion with a total audited score of 185 points, followed by Green Team (Berde Makausad) with 180 points, Yellow Team (Bibeni Luca) with 175 points, Blue Team (BIR) and Red Team (Pulang Ina) who tied with 170 points, and lastly, Orange Team (Ilocos Empanada) and Purple Team (Ooh-Lila) who both garnered 150 points. Furthermore, Blue Team (BIR) won for the best yell, while Red Team (Pulang Ina) claimed the best flag award.
According to Pink (cPINKa) team leader, Shakira Nicole B. Guintu, their victory came from proper planning before the activities and going all-in on collaboration. “No’ng nag-activity po kami, laging may nagsasalita and nakikinig. Pwedeng higher batch, pwedeng lower batch, basta lahat naman po kami nakikipag-cooperate,” said Guintu.
Guintu also added that the team building reflected real-world challenges in accountancy, helping them to be patient and to remain calm under pressure. “May activities na parang paulit-ulit na kami pero nire-remind namin na kalmahan lang, na i-go lang sa flow namin, sa pace namin,” the team leader stated.
The activity ultimately reinforced that future accountants must not only master numbers but also embody the 3 C’s—Collaborate, Compete, and Conquer—skills essential in navigating real-world professional engagements and organizational challenges. | via Justine Stephanie A. Canlas/The FOREFRONT

# Online Application for GC Admission Test Procedure (1st Semester, A.Y. 2024-2025)
[Image: Online Application for GC Admission Test Procedure (1st Semester, A.Y. 2024-2025)]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/)
**Monday, December 18, 2023**
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 1. CREATE ONLINE PORTAL ACCOUNT**
1.1 Go to Gordon College Admission Portal login page (<https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gca>)
1.2 Click “Create Account”
1.3 Type your email address, given name, last name, date of birth, contact number, desired password and select type of student (New Student, Transferee, Second Courser)
1.4 Click “Register”
Notes:
o A valid and existing email address is required in this process.
o Do not create multiple accounts as this will be a ground for disapproval of application.
· Do not forget your email and password
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 2. LOG ON TO YOUR ACCOUNT**
2.1 Go to the portal login page
2.2 Type your previously registered email address and password then click “Log In”
2.3 You will be directed to the portal Dashboard.
2.4 The Dashboard displays the status of your application.
[Image: 🟢]
 **STEP 3. PROFILING**
3.1 On the upper left side of the page, click “Upload Photo” to upload your recent photo.
Guide:
o 2×2 colored picture, white background
o Formal pose wearing collared top, No eyeglasses or any accessories like earrings.
o Picture should be taken not later than one week prior to filing of application.
3.2 On the Menu, click “Profile”
3.3 Completely and accurately fill out the forms (Personal Information, Family Background and Desired Programs)
3.4 Read the Gordon College General Data Privacy Notice and click the CHECKBOX.
3.5 Review the form and click “Submit”
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 4. UPLOADING OF DOCUMENTS**
4.1 On the Menu, click “Uploads”
4.2 Upload scanned or clear image of the required documents. Click the placeholder to browse for an image to upload.
4.3 The image file should be less than 2MB to avoid uploading errors
Required documents:
o Recent Good Moral Character Certificate
o PSA Authenticated Birth Certificate
o Senior High School ID or Any Valid ID Card
– Click “Submit” to finalize the uploading.
[Image: 🟢]
 **STEP 5. GCAT Schedule**
5.1 The schedule of GCAT will be posted to your portal account within three (3) working days after submission of COMPLETE application and required documents.
5.2 Upon posting of the test schedule on the online portal, you may download and print the following:
o GCAT Schedule slip
o Student Information Sheet
————————————————–
What to bring on the test day?
1. GCAT Schedule Slip
2. Student Information Sheets
3. Senior High School or any valid ID Card
5. Pencil and Pen
Note: Please be informed that all applications shall be submitted online to prevent the spread of Covid-19.

# Gordon College Joins National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill
[Image: Gordon College Joins National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, March 12, 2026**
LOOK | Students and personnel of Gordon College (GC) gathered earlier this afternoon, March 12, at Tapinac Oval Track as part of the National Simultaneous Earthquake Drill (NSED).
The earthquake drill simultaneously occurred at 3:30pm today in public and private institutions across the country to strengthen community resilience against a possible disaster.
NSED is practiced every quarter — totaling four times in a year — to establish nationwide earthquake preparedness. This is in preparation for possible earthquakes in the future including the “Big One”, a projected magnitude 7.2 earthquake predicted to hit Metro Manila and affect nearby provinces as the West Valley Fault is ripe for movement. | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Celebrates World Teachers’ Day
[Image: Gordon College Celebrates World Teachers’ Day]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, October 7, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN BLDG—The Office of the Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) hosted a World Teachers’ Day Celebration, together with various student organizations, which aims to honor the dedicated instructors of the institution on October 6, 2025, at Gordon College Main Bldg—Function Hall.
The event began with an opening prayer and remarks from Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, Gordon College President, and Dr. Darwin P. Paguio, Vice President for Academic Affairs.
“We cannot have a lavish lifestyle in teaching. But your dedication and passion will definitely [inaudible] through saying our students’ success— changing their lives, especially those who are underprivileged students, and you are making them a story,” said Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, showing her gratitude for the effort of Gordon College’s faculty.
Dr. Darwin P. Paguio also expressed to the faculty that the celebration isn’t just about their perseverance, but also to take care of all the hard work in the classrooms as they invest in nurturing curiosity that will nourish them for a lifetime.
After the opening ceremony, Performing Arts Students Ensemble (PASE) Voci tributed a chorale performance that honors the educators.
To spark joy with the event, the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Student Council, Midwifery Student Organization (MSO), and the Nursing Student Organization (NSO) hosted games to energize and entertain the teachers.
Furthermore, the PASE Ballo sparked warmth and embrace with a performance number.
Moreover, the Supreme Student Council (SSC) and Human Resource Management Unit (HRMU) conducted an awarding ceremony to appreciate the faculty’s skills with admiration and humor.
Meanwhile, Hon. Rolen Paulino Jr., City Mayor of Olongapo, held a speech and cash raffle for the faculty.
“Salamat sa sacrifices niyo, sa mahabang pasensiya ninyo, tiyaka sa lahat ng ginagawa niyo para sa mga students natin,” Paulino stated.
An Audio-Visual Presentation (AVP) video was shown to the faculty, featuring greetings and messages from the students, followed by the students who presented gifts to their beloved tutors.
Lastly, another raffle event was held with prizes for the faculty, which filled the room with joy and laughter.
The said event was supposed to be celebrated on October 3rd but was rescheduled due to Typhoon Paolo.| via Jasmine Zeah Llagas/The FOREFRONT

Thank you for being shining examples of sportsmanship, perseverance, and true school spirit. You represented Gordon College with heart, honor, and pride, and now, your efforts have been rewarded with well-deserved recognition:
Gordon College proudly achieved:
Sports Competition – 1st Runner-Up Overall
Cultural Competition – 2nd Runner-Up Overall
Our deepest gratitude to Hon. Rolen C. Paulino, City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Committee on Education Chairperson; Dr. Imelda D.P. Soriano, College President; Ms. Lovelyn P. Ceralde, Vice President for Student Welfare and Services; College Officials, Coaches, and all other personnel for their unwavering support in this endeavor.
Continue to rise, inspire, and conquer. The entire GC community is incredibly proud of you!

# Stude orgs answer GC’s SOAE call
[Image: Stude orgs answer GC’s SOAE call]
[Campus](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/campus/) [OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 26, 2025**
Despite the short attendance of student organizations and delegates in the GC 4th Student Organization Awards of Excellence (SOAE), the awarding ceremony proceeded in citing its outstanding student organizations and student-leaders in service for A.Y. 2024-2025 at GC Function Hall, yesterday.
The 4th SOAE awarded, in no particular order, its recipients of:
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization | Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP) – Gordon College Chapter, and Samahang Filipino (SamFil)
Top 3 Outstanding Student Organization Adviser | Jehan B. Doblado, Kristoffer Paul C. Obispo, and Mychale Williams
Top 3 Outstanding Community Extension Program | CHTM Student Council, Interactive Organization of Natural Sciences (IONS), and Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan
Meanwhile, Nicolas Milton Ching earned the Servant Student Leader of the Year while Klaudtrev Hanrick Payumo merited Rookie Student Servant of the Year. Jannae Mae De Jesus received the Student Organization Presidential Award and Sophia Joleen Evangelista with Outstanding Student Extensionist Award.
And in no particular order, this year’s Top 10 Outstanding Student Leaders were:
Nicolas Milton Ching | SPECS
Jhoemari Corpus | IONS
Janna Mae De Jesus | SMAP
La Arnie Morales | CHTM SC
Dhana Eunice Papa | JFINEX
Kharl Christopher Nofies | SAMFIL
Lorenzo Don Cayabyab | LTSP
Ralf Vincent Paule | PSCAS
John Kyle Perez | SAMFIL
Jay Angelo Manalang | LTSP
Aeros Raphael Morales | LELLS
Lynard Jay Ebalo | IONS
Mark Louis Macachor | CHTM SC
Sophia Joleen Evangelista | SMAP
Moreover, LTSP-GC Adviser Mychale Williams won the Student Organization Adviser of the Year.
The CHTM Student Council came out once more as the Student Council of the Year among others while IONS, after bagging two other awards earlier, also flexed on its Spirit of Service Award plaque while LTSP nagged the Student Organization of the Year title.
This ceremony is still in line with the celebration of the 26th Founding Anniversary of the institution.

**ONLINE ENLISTMENT FOR ENROLMENT PROCEDURE**
1. Log-on to the GCES portal: ([https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student](https://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fgordoncollegeccs.edu.ph%2Fgces%2Fstudent%3Ffbclid%3DIwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExYmtONXprRWlaY05XUDNlanNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR79DMwGOjURxgNtRNM48cNS14RZiQ03STk68zaq7Y7S38vvJxITV_-rwzIGXg_aem_7mI144fGqXu0N5q8QyMZdg&h=AT1IVkhOy2caj4FsOiZC6KtLcGS7CF_3aUEBr6YBiyDYrjeSV9jJmVnM3bvjtkLS9ubcybZBpCpmOIqF60ee1ATknqEkHUk7yk2IUuEBMgz49kgkahymCpB1C3ojTSon_CF7Xs8nSOkcRQ9O&__tn__=-UK-R&c[0]=AT0Td0sy4zKeSU-3h2cCfuXxcqiavVsIeutqEmIOq2vtU2iE4XHgcumSKzPZumwRz3xxrqzzZP5WhGb8FaQXp6B1x9EC7ds_pqrDxh3f4dMiTJO2HLZtbFeZeD65jIeH5Buamysrdisv8_WNYv-ZDzY71au_BX0n04ocFacFP6YxOgF_HZTOMNEUG9ApGJ7CxnI38dyCP3iXNW_PCf0UYA9JPA))
A “forgot password request” option is available in case of trouble logging in.
2. Check the Dashboard for the online clearance status.
You will not be able to proceed with the enlistment process until you are cleared of all accountabilities.
3. View your academic records in the following menu:
a. Term Grade
b. Summary of Grades
c. Academic Evaluation Sheet
4. Click Profile/Enlistment.
To request for change of information, visit the Office of the Registrar.
5. Update academic and enlistment information.
6. Select preferred courses to enroll.
7. Read the Gordon College General Data Privacy Notice and click the CHECKBOX.
8. Click “ENLIST” button.
9. Completing all the steps above will register you for enrolment.
Your schedule of classes will be posted in your GCES account on or before the start of classes. You may ask the assistance of your program coordinator for class schedule matters.

# Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings
[Image: Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 18, 2025**
We are proud to announce that Gordon College has officially earned a place in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Rankings 2025! Despite being a newcomer to the global stage, GC was ranked among the top 1,500+ out of 2,526 higher education institutions (HEIs) from 130 countries, assessed for their contributions to achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs)—a recognition based on universities’ social impact through research, stewardship, outreach, and teaching.
Gordon College participated in four SDG categories and earned the following standings:
• SDG 3 – Good Health and Well-being: Ranked 1001–1500
• SDG 4 – Quality Education: Ranked 1501+
• SDG 10 – Reduced Inequalities: Ranked 1001+
• SDG 17 – Partnerships for the Goals: Ranked 1501+
More than just recognition, this milestone reflects our evolving vision—to become a globally recognized local institution making meaningful contributions to global progress through inclusive, equitable, and sustainable development in higher education.
[Image: 🔗]

# GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025
[Image: GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, December 3, 2025**
The three-day program comes to an end as Gordon College (GC) conducts an exit conference for the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) last November 28, at the Gordon College Main Building, Function Hall.
Vying for one Level III program, five Level II programs, and two candidate programs this year, the institution’s big step is a leap for development, embodying character, excellence, and service along the way.
“This is not the end,” Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano opened the exit conference of this year’s accreditation with a firm statement.
The accreditors commended the institution’s strengths including the Library Ambassadors Organization (LAO), community extension programs, and professionalism.
Meanwhile, they also recommended improvements such as stronger programs to supplement both students’ and teachers’ needs, more tangible and sustainable community extensions, and subjects that focus on application rather than theories.
As Dr. Elizabeth Montero, the supervising accreditor said in her message, “The new oil now is consistency,” a reminder to stay true and stand into the lenses of continuous development where growth is evident and seen.
In response, Ms. Geraldine Raydanas and Mrs. Rowenalyn Sagun expressed their utmost gratitude and promised to improve in the areas mentioned.
Ending the program, Dr. Kristin Joy Mendoza, secretary of GC’s Board of Trustees, left a valuable statement regarding institutionalization and sustainability.
“Every accreditation is an opportunity to innovate and fill the spaces where we can do better,” Dr. Mendoza emphasized.
Meanwhile, the results of the 2025 accreditation are set to be announced in the next few weeks. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

It’s a wrap!
[Image: ✨]
The PRC On-Site Service Caravan at Gordon College was a resounding success, delivering a total of 549 services to Professionals in a single day. This initiative significantly improved access to essential PRC services within the local community—and is part of our ongoing commitment to support not only our Alumni but also to all Professionals.
Breakdown of Services Rendered:
• Renewal – 138
• Initial Registration – 60
• Duplicate – 11
• Certification – 170
• Authentication – 162
• Certificate of Special Order (COS) – 8
TOTAL – 549
A special acknowledgment to the dedicated PRC Committee for their exemplary service and leadership:
Ms. Elaine Perez Espela, Ms. Glenda B. Sapon (Team Leader), Ms. Joana Benigna Pabelonia, Mr. Joselito Dulay, and Mr. Angelo Pedro Hernandez.
We are also grateful to the student organizations for their invaluable support and active participation:
• College of Computer Studies Student Council (CCS SC)
• GCCCS Empowered League of Information Technology Education Students (ELITES)
Sincere appreciation goes to the VP for Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs, Dr. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, for her instrumental role in the successful implementation of this initiative.
We would also like to express our heartfelt thanks to Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing GCAAI President and Ms. Denise Lou Punzalan for spearheading this event, and to the Board of Trustees and Working Committee of the Gordon College Alumni Association Inc. (GCAAI) for their full support and commitment in making this initiative a success.
Special thanks to Dr. Imelda DP Soriano, College President, for her unwavering support in bringing services closer to the community.
This caravan is not just a service initiative—it’s our way of helping empower our alumni community as they continue their professional journeys.
Thank you to everyone who contributed to the success of the PRC On-Site Service Caravan. Together, we continue to broaden access to professional services and empower our community.

# GCAAI Membership
#### Qualifications for Membership
A. The qualifications for membership to the Association are either:
1. Holder of academic degree from Gordon College (formerly Olongapo City Colleges)
2. Holder of secondary/basic education diploma from Gordon College-Senior High School
3. Holder of academic title or postgraduate diploma from Gordon College (formerly Olongapo City Colleges)
4. Not having been dismissed for misconduct/offenses or scholastic/academic deficiency which is automatically considered not a member of the Association.
B. Membership to the association may be bestowed upon the approval of the GC-AA Board of Trustees:
1. Holders of honorary degrees from the College;
2. Former or current members of the faculty and staff of the College who have rendered service to the College for at least ten (10) years.
C. Active members are those who have qualified as members under Section 1 and 2, and are either:
1. Members who have paid lifetime membership dues;
2. Members who have paid and are up-to-date in their annual membership dues;
3. Members who have participated in such other programs that sustain, develop, and promote the Association’s objectives as determined by the Board.
D. The Alumni Oath shall be recited by a member who signifies its willingness to be part of and reflect its allegiance to the Association. The Alumni Oath shall be administered by the President or its duly authorized representative in a ceremony, commencement and/or event. The Alumni Oath in its current form is as follows:
*I **(state your name)**,*  
*a graduate of Gordon College,*  
*hereby undertake to*  
*vigilantly upholds the ideals*  
*and shield the honor of my Alma Mater,*  
*and as a fulfilled member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc.,*  
*will uphold its Constitution and By-Laws,*  
*to remain a true paragon towards excellence,*  
*committed to do justice to everyone*  
*in service to our country and our God.*  
*So help me, God!*

# THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025
[Image: THE 4TH GORDON COLLEGE DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD: Call for Nomination 2025]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/)
**Monday, August 4, 2025**
**GENERAL INFORMATION AND GUIDELINES**
Pursuant to Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) Board Resolution No. 4, series of 2022, the GCAAI establishes the DISTINGUISHED ALUMNI SERVICE AWARD (DASA) to honor Gordon College graduates who have excelled both in their professional and personal careers and who have served as an example to other alumni, students, and the community, both locally and globally. Starting this year, the GCAAI Board of Trustees will recognize the exemplary service and distinguished achievements that brought honor to the Institution and have made significant contributions to society. The GC-DASA recipients will exemplify the core values that the Institution uphold since its establishment: Exceptional Character, Remarkable Excellence, And Unselfish Service.  
The GC-DASA shall be the highest honor bestowed by the Alumni Association and will be an event to be celebrated annually as part of the GC Founding Anniversary and/or GC Alumni Homecoming.  
**GENERAL GUIDELINES:**
1. Only graduates of Gordon College (or GC, formerly Olongapo City Colleges) are eligible for the GC-DASA. The nominee shall be a member of the Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. as confirmed by its Board.
2. Any GC graduate, faculty member, or stakeholders may make nominations for the GC-DASA. Self–  
   nominations are accepted. The nominee shall accomplish the Official Nomination Form to be provided by the GCAAI. No limitation will be placed on the total number of nominations accepted for consideration.
3. The nominee shall have no pending administrative or criminal case upon nomination. He/she shall exhibit a high degree of character & integrity.
4. Only one recipient per college/program will be awarded. In case the college/program will have no  
   nominees/recipients, the awarding ceremony will still commence. Likewise, the GCAAI Board of Trustees can directly, without prejudice to the respective college, nominate and evaluate for such an award though the college/program have already a candidate.
5. Only living candidates are considered. In the event of the death of a candidate between notification of the award and the award ceremony, the award may be given posthumously with the permission of the family and the GCAAI. In the event that the awardee declines to accept the award and/or fails to attend the awarding ceremonies, the GCAAI reserves the right to withdraw the award.
6. A GC graduate sitting as a member of the selection committee or a member of the GCAAI Board of Trustees is not eligible to receive the award. This individual is eligible for nomination after a one-year waiting period from the date he/she steps down from his/her role.
7. Recipients are only eligible for one GC-DASA in a lifetime.
8. The recipients will receive a plaque or certificate of recognition and will be posted on the official FB page of the GCAAI. It will announce the annual recipients through various methods and honor each recipient with the appropriate recognition.
9. If for any reason, the GCAAI is not able to conduct the Awards as planned (including due to tampering, unauthorized intervention, fraud, technical difficulties, pandemic, or any other causes beyond the control of the organizer which impact the administration, security, fairness, integrity or proper conduct of the Awards), then they reserve the right in their sole discretion to make any reasonable variation to the Awarding Ceremony.
10. The Nomination Committee and/or GCAAI Board of Trustees may disqualify any nominee that they  
    reasonably determine has breached any of the terms and conditions or otherwise engaged in unlawful or other improper conduct that may bring the Awards into disrepute or jeopardize the fair and proper conduct of the Awards.
11. Other guidelines may be promulgated as decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees.
**NOMINATION COMMITTEE:**  
The Nomination Committee (NC) may be organized by the Dean/OIC in every college and/or the GCAAI. The committee must be composed of at least three (3) members from their respective colleges. The total count of committee members should be an odd number. The chairperson of the NC shall be the Dean or its duly authorized representative and for the GCAAI, any member can initiate such nomination. No member of the NC must be related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the nominees/candidates for awards. If the Dean/OIC is related within the second degree of consanguinity or affinity to any of the candidates for awards, the Dean/OIC must inhibit him/herself from participating in the process. The approval shall come from the person next in rank.  
The NC shall:
1. Abide by the criteria set forth by the GCAAI.
2. For nominees endorsed by the College, it shall issue a certificate of authenticity to all the documents that will be submitted to the GCAAI.
3. Deliberate on the qualifications of the nominees for the awards based on the criteria and the documents submitted.
4. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the result of nomination for approval.
5. Recommend to the GCAAI Board of Trustees or its Alumni Association President the resolution on any related issue that may arise from the results of the awards.
6. Ensure that the guidelines stipulated herein are followed.
**NOMINATION AND SELECTION PROCESS:**
1. Complete the online/hard copy Nomination Form.
2. Nominators will receive an auto-generated copy via email of what they submitted, i.e., the nomination form including any attachments. In any case, it is not available, the NC may determine what means of notification it will conduct to the nominators to confirm the submission of the nomination form and its attachments.
3. Nominees and nominators will be contacted by the NC/GCAAI within three days of receipt of the  
   nomination to acknowledge receipt.
4. A nominee may be asked to clarify or submit further information and evidence to assist the NC/GCAAI in assessing the nomination at any stage of the selection process.
5. The NC/GCAAI may also seek verification of the nominee’s achievements from their current or previous employer or other relevant individual or organization representative. By being nominated for this Award, the nominees are giving consent to the NC/GCAAI to contact these individuals to verify nominations.
6. Nominations will be reviewed and shortlisted by the NC/GCAAI and nominees who have not been  
   shortlisted will be notified. The GC-DASA candidates will be asked to submit a brief profile for this purpose. The candidate shall garner a total score of 60 points to be included in the shortlist. If a nominee cannot meet the required points, he/she is automatically excluded from the shortlist.
7. Every member of the NC will recommend/endorse the final candidate to the GCAAI President and then, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, sitting as Awards Selection Committee, will issue a confirmation through a Board Resolution. The report on the results of the NC shall be signed by all members of the committee and certified by the chairperson.
8. In any case that such protest will be filed during the confirmation of the final candidates, the final decision on the protest shall be decided by the GCAAI Board of Trustees. The GCAAI Board of Trustees’ decision is final on all matters relating to the nomination process and the selection of award recipients, and they are not required to provide feedback as to why a nomination was unsuccessful.

# Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
[Image: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy]
**Friday, August 8, 2025**
Instructions for Gordon College Students: Clean As You Go & Bring Your Own Trash Policy
Purpose:
To foster a clean and respectful campus environment while promoting sustainability through personal responsibility for waste.
Before Class or Activity:
1. Prepare Your Materials:
– Bring Your Own Trash: Carry a small, reusable bag or container to collect any waste you generate during class or activities. This could be a sturdy tote, a lunch bag, or any suitable container.
– Plan for Snacks/Drinks: If you plan to consume food or beverages, pack them in a way that minimizes waste (e.g., reusable containers, bottles).
During Class or Activity:
2. Maintain Your Space:
– Keep Your Area Clean: Throughout the session, stay vigilant about your surroundings. Avoid leaving papers, wrappers, or any personal items strewn about.
– Immediate Clean-Up: If you create a mess (accidental spills, paper cuts, etc.), address it promptly. If cleaning supplies are available, use them to tidy up immediately.
3. Responsible Waste Management:
– Use Your Reusable Container: Whenever you have waste, place it in your reusable bag or container. Keep it nearby for easy access.
– Respect Shared Spaces: Be mindful of not just your space but also those around you. Dispose of any common area waste (e.g., napkins from community snacks) in your own trash.
After Class or Activity:
4. Final Cleanup:
– Inspect Your Area:
Before leaving, take a moment to ensure your seating area and any communal spaces (tables, chairs) are tidy.
– Empty Your Trash into Appropriate Bins: When you leave the classroom or activity, empty your reusable container at designated trash or recycling bins outside the classroom (if available). Take anything that belongs to you with you.
5. Dispose Responsibly:
– Recycle When Possible: If your waste is recyclable, place it in the recycling bin. Be sure to check the college’s recycling guidelines to ensure proper disposal.
Additional Responsibilities:
6. Encourage a Culture of Cleanliness:
– Remind Peers:If you notice a classmate leaving a mess, gently remind them or offer to help.
– Lead by Example: Demonstrating your commitment to the “Clean As You Go” policy can inspire your peers to do the same.
7. Feedback and Improvement:
– Participate in discussions or feedback sessions organized by the college to share your insights on how the Clean As You Go policy is working.
8. Engage with Initiatives:
– Stay updated on any college-led environmental initiatives or clean-up days, and participate actively when you can.
By adhering to these instructions, you contribute to creating a cleaner, more sustainable campus environment while developing valuable habits of personal responsibility and community stewardship. Thank you for doing your part!

# Empowering GFPS members through the Application of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines
[Image: Empowering GFPS members through the Application of Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, June 17, 2025**
The Gordon College, through its Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Unit as the GC-GFPS Secretariat, successfully facilitated a seminar for members of the Gordon College Gender and Development Focal Point System (GC-GFPS) entitled “Empowering GFPS Members through the Application of the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines” on June 13, 2025, held at the GC Library Audio-Visual Room.
This capacity-building initiative was organized to further strengthen the College’s gender mainstreaming efforts. It aimed to equip GC-GFPS members with the essential knowledge and practical skills needed to effectively integrate gender perspectives into the institution’s programs, projects, and activities. The seminar primarily focused on the application of the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG)—a critical framework designed to ensure that development initiatives are gender-responsive and aligned with the overarching goals of gender equality and women’s empowerment.

# GC Library Renovation: 40% Done
[Image: GC Library Renovation: 40% Done]
[LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, August 19, 2024**
The Gordon College library is currently undergoing renovation project which aims at transforming the space into a more dynamic and student-friendly environment.
In an interview with The Forefront, Mr. Marlon Atencio, the college librarian, provided detailed updates and valuable insights into the objectives, current developments, and anticipated benefits of the project for students and faculty alike.
According to Mr. Atencio, the upgraded library is shifting towards a “learning commons” setup.
“Kasi yung college library natin ang mangyayari ay parang learning commons na siya, kumbaga parang café setup,” he shared.
In addition, the college librarian announced plans to enhance the library by installing vending machines, similar to those at ground floor, allowing students to enjoy snacks—though only solid foods, other snacks, will be permitted, with liquids excluded.
He further mentioned that the timeline for the renovation’s completion remains flexible.
“Wala naman binigay na timeline, ang gusto lang manyari is as soon as possible matapos siya para siyempre magamit na ito ng mga estudyante.” he added
As of now, the renovation is around 40% complete. Key areas such as the computer section and audiovisual room are being redesigned with high-quality materials.
Moreover, he also shared that various areas, such as the flooring are already underway and the renovation is focusing on upgrading both the structural and aesthetic aspects of the library to ensure a premium experience for GCians.
Students and faculty can look forward to an enhanced library experience. The space are expected to be more conducive to learning with upgraded seating, including sofas and modern furniture.
Furthermore, the college librarian also mentioned that they should not only expect the library to be well-maintained but also that its services will be improved to ensure that students can use it effectively.
In addressing the specific needs of students and faculty, the library will host separate orientation sessions to familiarize students with the new setup.
“Kaya hindi kami nakisabay sa student orientation kasi magkakaroon kami ng separate orientation sa library, wherein ang gagawin namin ay mag-kakaroon ng library tour ang bawat section, para alam nila kung paano gamitin lahat ng area, para maiwasan din natin [na] nahihirapan kung paano i-access [ang] mga gamit, pati mga electronics resources lahat ‘yon it-tackle natin during orientation,” he explains.
Next, he also discussed changes in the physical layout, such as new glass doors and relocating the lockers outside the library.
Additionally, he encouraged students to reach out through the library’s Facebook page for online inquiries.
Once completed, the renovated Gordon College library promises to be a welcoming and efficient learning space for all that fosters both learning and collaboration.

# GC ALAB 2026: Connecting Job Seekers to Opportunities on GC’s 27th Anniversary
[Image: GC ALAB 2026: Connecting Job Seekers to Opportunities on GC’s 27th Anniversary]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, February 25, 2026**
In celebration of the 27th Founding Anniversary, the GC ALAB Job Fair was successfully conducted on February 25, 2026, at Ayala Malls Harbor Point – Activity Center from 9:00 AM to 3:00 PM.
The event was spearheaded by the Public Employment Service Office (PESO) in collaboration with Gordon College, particularly under the Community Extension Unit and the Office of Student Welfare and Services, as part of the institution’s commitment to serving its stakeholders by providing accessible employment opportunities.
A total of 30 participating companies joined the job fair, offering various career opportunities for job seekers and fresh graduates.
This meaningful activity was made possible through the partnership of Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), SBMA Labor, Ayala Malls Harbor Point, JPMAP, SMAP, and the College of Business and Accountancy.
The GC ALAB Job Fair reflects Gordon College’s dedication to community extension and workforce development.

# GCian Alumni Makes Name on OSLP 2023
[Image: GCian Alumni Makes Name on OSLP 2023]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, October 11, 2023**
Mr. Marko L. Aratea and Mr. Nieki E. Garigo, both alumni from Gordon College’s College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS), stand out as finalists during the award ceremony for Outstanding Student Leaders of the Philippines (OSLP) 2023 held at Teacher’s Camp, Baguio City, on October 1, 2023.
In an interview with The Forefront, Aratea and Garigo claimed that the Office of Student Welfare and Service (OSWS) and GC-CEAS had announced that they would represent the institution in the aforementioned competition and directed all requirements to Transforming Advocates and Champions (TRAC).
“I was astonished and exhilarated when it was announced that I was one of the top 20 finalists. Knowing that out of 135 applicants across our nation and at various colleges and universities, I was chosen. For me, I consider that one of the great achievements that I had,” Aratea said, reflecting on how he felt when he knew that he was one of the finalists.
While Garigo stated, “Syempre napasigaw ako that time, because bago pa dumating sa point na yan ay marami kaming dinaanang proseso at screening. I still remember the day where they conducted our final interview and sakto pang nakaduty ako sa Cooperating School ko for my practice teaching, and as in, medyo mahina ang signal niyan kaya during my 1-hour interview, ay naroroon ako sa ilalim ng manga ng Regional Science High School para maging stable ang internet, and sobrang naging successful naman ng interview dahil every time na may mga ganyang screening, kasi ako I always put in my mind na parang “kachikahan” ko lang ang interviewer at sobrang ganda ng discussion namin, especially yung point na may SDG pa sa question, like I am not ready that time dahil ang nasa isip ko talaga niyan ang lesson sa susunod na subject na tuturuan ko. But thank God because everything went well.”
They expressed their deepest gratitude to TRAC for the opportunity they attained and to their co-finalists from different schools. The representatives would also like to thank Gordon College, the GC Alumni Association, Inc., GC-RDCES, GC-OSWS, GC-CEAS, their families, and lastly, the Almighty God.
“Be who you are, be true to yourself, and always know your advocacy by heart and mind. Think of this competition as a way for you to raise your voice as a student leader, an advocate, and a champion,” Aratea said, while Garigo stated, “Be a leader na hindi tuta; be a leader na boses ng mag-aaral; be a leader who is pro-people, pro-student, and pro-community,” as their advice for those aspiring student leaders of the institution.
Aratea was also hailed as the champion for the Student Research Paper Presentation at the said event.

# 𝐎𝐒𝐖𝐒, 𝐏𝐄𝐒𝐎, 𝐃𝐎𝐋𝐄 𝐤𝐢𝐜𝐤 𝐨𝐟𝐟 𝐋𝐄𝐆𝐒 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓
[Image: 𝐎𝐒𝐖𝐒, 𝐏𝐄𝐒𝐎, 𝐃𝐎𝐋𝐄 𝐤𝐢𝐜𝐤 𝐨𝐟𝐟 𝐋𝐄𝐆𝐒 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟓]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, May 29, 2025**
The Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) in partnership with the Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE) and Public Employment Service Office (PESO), conducted their annual Labor Education for Graduating Students (LEGS) 2025 yesterday, May 28, at the SMX Convention Center Olongapo.
The morning session included students from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) assisted by the Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan (SMAP).
Meanwhile, the event continued until afternoon for the graduating students of College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS), College of Computer Studies (CCS), and College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS).
Started by Mr. Arvin Fabian, Senior Labor Employment Officer of DOLE, the seminar aims to equip its participants with sufficient knowledge regarding labor standards.
“Ang mandate po kasi ng DOLE is to ensure that everyone receive[s] what is due to them,” assured Mr. Fabian.
Additionally, this event was led by Ms. Jehan Doblado, Career Education and Job Placement Services Unit (CEJPSU) Coordinator, with the goal of preparing graduating studentsfor the corporate world.
“Para hindi sila zero knowledge. [Para] pagka-graduate nila alam nila ang gagawin nila,” the CEJPSU Coordinator said.
In her opening remarks, Ms. Lovelyn Ceralde, the Vice President of the Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS), stressed how necessary it is to study these standards.
“It is important that you, our future leaders and contributors, are well-versed in these fundamental principles,” said Ms. Ceralde.
She also highlighted the upcoming Commencement Exercises in July for the graduating batch 2025.
“See you on July 9 with your black togas!” the OSWS Vice President noted.
In an interview with The Forefront, some participants from different departments shared their insights on how the LEGS 2025 helped them as a graduating student of Gordon College.
“Ang laking tulong nitong seminar saken as a graduating student para maging prepared sa mga kakailanganin ko as a first time job seeker,” Jordan Magbanua, CCS Graduating Student claimed.
“Very helpful para sa mga graduating students lalo na sa akin kasi sa personal experience ko na naging employee na nag-resign para magpatuloy ang pag-aaral. Na-enlighten ako sa seminar na ito,” CEAS Graduating Student said.
Moreover, this event eyes recurrence every year in face-to-face set-up to ensure that graduating students remain well-informed regarding their rights as current or future employees.

# 𝐈𝐬𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨 𝐚𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐤𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐚𝐦𝐮𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐨, 𝐛𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐤𝐭𝐢𝐛𝐨
[Image: 𝐈𝐬𝐚𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨 𝐚𝐭 𝐈𝐧𝐤𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐚𝐦𝐮𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐨, 𝐛𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐠𝐲𝐚𝐧 𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐚𝐠𝐨𝐧𝐠 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐤𝐭𝐢𝐛𝐨]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, July 18, 2025**
Pinaglaanan ng panibagong pananaw ang mga kasalukuyang student leader ng Dalubhasaan Gordon patungkol sa paksa ng pamumuno bilang lider sa katatapos na Capacity Building and Personality Development Program for Student Organizations 2025 na ginanap sa Function 1, SMX Olongapo City Convention Center.
Ang naturang seminar na may temang: “Leadership Rooted in Purpose: Strengthening Self, Serving Others, Shaping Tomorrow” ay dinaluhan ng dalawang resource speaker mula Central Luzon State University (CLSU) na sina Dr. Irene G. Bustos, CLSU Student Affairs and Services Dean at ni Ms. Joan B. Pelagio, CLSU Paruparong Bukid Adviser.
Ang nabanggit na talakayan ay pinasimulan ni Dr. Bustos sa kanyang presentasyon tungkol sa kung paano nga ba ang tamang pamumuno ng isang lider sa kanyang nasasakupan at mga kalidad na dapat niyang tinataglay. Binigyang pansin din ni Dr. Bustos kung paano gawing reyalidad ang bisyon ng isang lider.
Sa kabilang banda, binuksan naman ni Ms. Joan B. Pelagio, CLSU Paruparong Bukid Adviser, ang diskusyong hinggil sa pagiging gender-responsive at pagbuo ng isang inklusibong kapaligiran at sa loob ng isang institusyon.
Ayon kay Ms. Pelagio, sa pamamagitan ng gender-responsiveness at inklusibong pakikitungo, mas makakabuo tayo ng pagkakaisa, respeto, at ligtas kapaligiran.
“Nag-start kami with few so with support and respect na hindi kami hinahayaan kung hindi sinusuportahan kami na gawin kung ano yung mga activities na kailangan, ‘yun yung isa sa nagmo-motivate sa group o sa org para ituloy yung advocacy.” Saad ni Ms. Pelagio ukol sa lagay ng Paruparong Bukid sa kanilang institusyon.
Si Ms. Pelagio ang tagapayo ng Paruparong Bukid, isang organisasyong nabuo sa isang obserbasyon at pagsasaliksik nina Dr. Bustos patungkol sa homophobia at transphobia na siyang naging pasimundan ng MAYARI na organisayon naman ng LGBTQIA+ ng Dalubhasaang Gordon.
Sa kabuuan ng nasabing seminar, “If you have passion for change, keep that passion fired, ika nga, [and] lighted, and pursue your vision and make it a reality. Be aware of everything that happens within and outside yourself. And kung anuman ang mga challenges, mga issues, problems ‘no parte lahat ito ng pagiging leader kaya hold on and keep the fire burning — yung passion mo to lead and to make change for the betterment of your studentry, the community, and siyempre the nation as a whole.” Ani Dr. Bustos sa mga kasalukuyang student leader at nagnanais na maging lider sa hinaharap.

# “2,000 change-makers” attend NSTP grad ‘25
[Image: “2,000 change-makers” attend NSTP grad ‘25]
[NSTP](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/nstp/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, June 8, 2025**
Two thousand Gordon College (GC) students enrolled in their National Service and Training Program (NSTP) courses filled the SMX Convention Center, Olongapo City, on Wednesday, for this year’s NSTP Graduation Ceremony.
With the theme, “Volunteerism in Action: Shaping Leaders, Strengthening Community”, this year’s batch of NSTP graduates was graced by Miss Manila 2014 Dr. Krischelle D. Halili.
Dr. Halili emphasized that leadership is an “act not for yourself, but for others” in her address. “Serve something greater than yourself and take your friends with you.”
Moreover, she called on students to love the country and God while also channeling ‘tech natives’ to participate in online community initiatives as well as community services and university council activities on-site.
Leading the initial confirmation of the graduates, Vice President for Academic Affairs Dr. Darwin P. Paguio also delivered his special message, focusing on the impact of genuine leadership.
“True leadership is not defined merely by title or accolades but by the impact you make in the lives of others,” he said as he congratulated the “two thousand change-makers” afterward.
This was followed by the awarding of certificates to Literary Training Service (LTS) and Civic Welfare Training Service (CWTS) completers from the different departments of the institution.
NSTP Instructor Mr. Hubert Lew Magrata delivered his closing remarks to officially close the program.

# LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon
[Image: LCUAA 2025 Welcomes Rainy Sunday Afternoon]
[LCUAA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/lcuaa/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, October 19, 2025**
The eagerly anticipated week-long 14th LCUAA National Games officially kicked off today at the City of San Jose del Monte (CSJDM) Sports Complex.
Cheers and applause filled the venue as the opening ceremony began with a parade featuring all 27 participating colleges, each led by their institution’s gold medalists.
Following the parade was the introduction of the LCUAA 2025 representative officers, a doxology by the Sining Tanglawan ng San Jose del Monte Performing Group, the singing of the National Anthem and the San Jose del Monte city hymn, and an energetic dance performance by the San Joseño World Class Talents troupe.
City College of San Jose del Monte (CCSJDM) College President Dr. Roger S. Ambida delivered the opening remarks, warmly welcoming delegates to the institution.
Hon. Arturo B. Robes then gave the keynote address, encouraging athletes to build a brighter and more empowered future.
Additionally, Dr. Lora L. Yusi, CHED Region 3 Director IV, gave an inspiring speech, highlighting the significance of LCUAA 2025 as a testament to the growth of local universities: “We see scholars who dream larger than themselves.”
The competitive events officially begin tomorrow, October 20, 2025.
Stay tuned to the official Facebook page of The Forefront for updates on the 14th LCUAA National Games. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara / The FOREFRONT

# LIMC History
Gordon College Library started in 1999 when the former Olongapo City Colleges, now Gordon College, put up a curricular program for Computer Studies and Business Management and Accountancy. With only a few books and mostly computer subjects in hand, Mrs. Remedios Sapuan, who was then a Utility worker, took charge of the library operation with the assistance of the Administrative Staff.
The Library moved four times to different locations in the building and its fourth was in the former KHG Hall. When the library was first opened, books were in the cabinet with glass doors and were locked. The library was using the close shelf system, where stacks cannot be directly accessed by users. The students should approach the assigned staff when borrowing books and it was then the bookshelf was opened to get the book needed.
In 2002, Thelma Ramos, a clerk aid of the Assessor’s Office, was transferred to the College Library to assist Mrs. Sapuan in the library operation. According to Mrs. Sapuan, a certain instructor, in the person of Mr. Sydney Maglalang, was temporarily assigned to the library. Services of the staff in the City Library were borrowed in the processing of books acquired for the College Library. In June 2003, the first College Librarian was hired, Ms. Maria Corazon D. Maneja as a casual position. Later she was promoted to a permanent position. The problem was by her was the book organization, the right classification of books, classification tools (DDC) Dewey Decimal Classification System and there were no catalog cards and cabinets. Some of the books were infested by termites which were already problems before but were not treated completely. Books were thenr eorganized, classified, and cataloged and were assigned new numbers using DD Classification tools that were borrowed from the Ci4ty Library. The circulation counter and grills dividing the books stacks and reading area were removed. Shelves were distributed and fastened on all library walls to make the books accessible to the users and to widen the reading area. Heavily termite-infested books were removed and deleted from the records. New books were formed. Newspapers were compiled with the date labeled and put on display. In 2006, books from Australia were received by the library through the Rotary Club of Olongapo. Books were found to be outdated and mostly for High School and Elementary level students. In the same year, another book donation was received from Asia Foundations, Inc. Books became very useful to patrons because of their general content. In March 2007, Ms. Betty Fielder, a friend of Mrs. Dely Aristorenas, former College Administrator, donated books and magazines for use by HRM students and faculty. Two air conditioners were installed to replace the former electric fans for ventilation and besides its small space, library patrons flocked the library especially nursing students who occupied the most number of enrollees. The library had then 8,361 volumes of books as of October 2007.
In the summer of 2010, the librarian during one of the EXECOM (ExecutiveCommittee) meetings, suggested removing over duplicated copies of books to provide spaces for new arrivals. Enough copies from weeded books were retained. 2,958 books were discarded and returned to GSO (Government Supply Office) but the latter suggested just donating them to the City Library.
In 2012, Gordon College's 13th Founding Anniversary, a Give-a-Book Project was launched and spearheaded by the College Library to collect tertiary level books from students, faculty, alumni, and friends of Gordon College. As of this date, May 6, 2013, we have collected 182 book donations.
On August 7, 2012, when the first knee-deep flood entered the College Library, books located on the first two-row level shelves were partially damaged but were restored. But on September 23, 2013, during a chest-deep heavy flood of Olongapo City, almost 2/3 of the library materials were soaked in the mud and water and devastated and discarded. Furniture which was made of light materials, files, and newly acquired books was included, but other computers were restored. Due to the kindness of City Mayor Rolen C. Paulino, he approved a 1 million budget for the purchase of new general education books, professional books, and journals. The first set of books was delivered in December 2013. Another era was opened for Gordon College Library for its transfer to the new building funded and built by SM in 2015-2016. The official transfer began on June 24, 2016, until July of the same year. The new and much wider library which is located on the fourth floor was opened to students on August 1, 2016, the start of the first semester, the Academic Year 2016- 2017.
Mr. Marlon A. Atencio recommended to the Executive Council the change of the library name into “Library and Instructional Media Center” for the reason that the library is now having a media center that supports the educational needs of the library and it was approved by the Executive Council spearheaded by the College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano last May 31, 2018.

# Beauty Meets Sportsmanship: Pislaan and Pradas Crowned as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025
[Image: Beauty Meets Sportsmanship: Pislaan and Pradas Crowned as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY— Cheers filled the East Tapinac Oval Grounds as the perfect blend of charm and athletic spirit came to life with representatives from various colleges showcasing their poise and sportsmanship, on the recently concluded Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest 2025 on Monday, September 15.
Kick-starting off the event with a bang, representatives wowed the crowd with their charisma and creative display of athletic-themed fashion for the Best in Sportswear award, which went to Mr. Chester Pislaan and Ms. Bhavjeet Kaur L. Singh from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors.
But the night didn’t end there—the battle for the ultimate title pushed the candidates to bring out their best poise, personality, and confidence. As the event drew to a close, the stage erupted in cheers and anticipation as the overall winners were finally announced.
After an intense showdown, Ms. Dhana Del C. Pradas from the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards and Mr. Chester Pislaan from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) emerged as Mr. and Ms. Sportsfest—proving that talent, wit, and stage presence shine brightest when matched with determination and sportsmanship.
Furthermore, hot on their heels, the 1st Runner-Up titles went to Mr. Marc Anthony Acula (CBA) Wizards and Ms. Bhavjeet Kaur L. Singh (CEAS) Mentors, whose outstanding performances made them strong contenders throughout the competition.
Meanwhile, Mr. Krizztan Vic A. Dominico and Ms. Ron Eriele Joi P. Agustin from College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers secured the 2nd Runner-Up spot, earning admiration for their dynamic partnership.
In 3rd place, Mr. Charles De Leon from College of Computer Studies (CCS) Phoenix and Ms. Alexandra Macky Alisbo from College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) claimed their rightful spot. At the same time, Mr. Jack Francis P. Meran (CHTM) Sharks and Ms. Janna Lyn Rolls, CCS Phoenix, completed the roster as the 4th place finishers. | via Love Lorainne Venus Acedo/The FOREFRONT

# WARRIORS’ CARNAGE: GC Bags Second Championship Streak at LCUAA National Games
[Image: WARRIORS’ CARNAGE: GC Bags Second Championship Streak at LCUAA National Games]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 25, 2025**
After six days of grueling competition, the Gordon College (GC) Warriors are hailed as the ultimate victors of the 14th Local Colleges and Universities Athletic Association (LCUAA) National Games, marking GC’s second winning streak as the overall champion.
Across several categories, the Warriors collectively bagged 31 gold, 22 silver, and 24 bronze medals across their LCUAA stint, a resounding sweep above overall 2nd place Kolehiyo ng Lungsod ng Lipa’s (KLL) 23 gold, 18 silver, and 21 bronze medal tally.
Meanwhile, the overall 3rd place title was claimed by Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig (PLP) with 15 gold, 13 silver, and 15 bronze medals across their run.
Furthermore, GC brought home championship plaques in the swimming, dancesport, and taekwondo categories awarded today, October 25, at the CSJDM Sports Complex. The Olongapo-reps were also awarded plaques for 1st runner-up in badminton, chess, and men’s futsal, as well as; 2nd runner-up in athletics.
The Warriors are set to defend their winning streak at next year’s national games at Binoloan, Pangasinan, hosted by the University of Eastern Pangasinan (UEP). | via Lance Isaac Leon/The FOREFRONT

# OFFICIAL STATEMENT FROM GORDON COLLEGE
[Image: OFFICIAL STATEMENT FROM GORDON COLLEGE]
**Sunday, January 4, 2026**
Gordon College is aware of the report aired by Bilyonaryo News in January 2026 concerning an alleged ₱19 million overpayment related to the implementation of the Free Higher Education (FHE) program specifically the mismatched definition of laboratory rooms.
In connection with the Commission on Audit’s (COA) rejoinder dated March 14, 2025, regarding Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) No. CHED-CO-2025-003 concerning the Free Higher Education (FHE) reimbursements to Gordon College for Academic Years 2019-2020 to 2023-2024, the institution submitted its clarifications and justification in coordination with the UniFAST Secretariat and Commission on Audit last May 16, 2025.
The institution’s initial inclusion of the Audio-Visual Room (AVR) fees under laboratory fees was made with good intentions and full transparency. This long-standing policy was openly reflected in the institution’s submitted and approved Tuition and Other School Fees (TOSF), which listed the AVR for laboratory fees. However as early as last year, once informed that “laboratory fees” is strictly interpreted to relate to specific courses that use specialized equipment, which the previous classification may not have fully met, the College adjusted accordingly and removed the said fees from the list.
The College wishes to clarify that funds received under the Free Higher Education program are meticulously allocated and budgeted which undergoes strict audit by appropriate national government agencies, including the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) and UniFAST and the Commission on Audit. All collections approved by the Commission on Higher Education (CHED) – UniFAST were allocated transparently for the operations and development of the college.
Gordon College is fully cooperating with concerned government agencies and complying with the recommendations of the Commission on Audit. The institution has always upheld transparency, accountability, and the proper stewardship of public funds. We respectfully call on the public to allow due process to take its course and to rely on verified information from official sources. Gordon College remains committed to addressing this matter responsibly and will provide updates as soon as official findings are available.

# GC: Shortlisted for Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year (THE) Awards Asia 2025
[Image: GC: Shortlisted for Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year (THE) Awards Asia 2025]
[IPDEA](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ipdea/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, April 24, 2025**
Gordon College is absolutely thrilled and incredibly proud to announce that we are one of the 8 finalists in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Awards Asia 2025 under the category of Technological or Digital Innovation of the Year! “This award recognizes the innovative use of digital technology to improve any function at a university, be that at institutional, departmental or faculty level”.
This incredible recognition is for our pioneering Gordon College Learners’ Academic Management Portal (GCLAMP), a testament to our commitment to innovative and accessible education for our students.
We extend our deepest gratitude and congratulations to the entire Gordon College community, especially to the brilliant minds behind GCLAMP. Our College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, our Vice President for Institutional Planning, Development and External Affairs, Ms. Kristin Joy A. Mendoza, our Vice President for Administration and Finance and Project Leader of GCLAMP, Dr. Erlinda S. Casela-Abarintos, and our Coordinator of the International Affairs Unit, Ms. Precious S. De Castro, proudly represented Gordon College at the awards ceremony and the Asia Universities Summit in Macau, China from April 22-24, 2025.
A special kudos to everyone who has been part of the GCLAMP journey: the Commission on Higher Education Regional Office III (CHEDROIII) headed by Dr. Lora L. Yusi, our supportive City Mayor, Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., the Chairperson of the Committee on Education, Hon. Cristabelle C. Paulino, the dedicated GCLAMP development team, all our hardworking college personnel, and our valued stakeholders. Your collective efforts and support have been instrumental in this achievement!
This milestone reflects Gordon College’s dedication to pushing boundaries and embracing technology to enhance the learning experience. We are honored to be recognized among the leading institutions in Asia.

[Image: 𝐈𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐍𝐨𝐰 𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐂𝐚𝐦𝐩𝐮𝐬: 𝐆𝐂 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐝𝐮𝐜𝐞𝐬 𝐒𝐎𝐆𝐈𝐄-𝐈𝐧𝐜𝐥𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐔𝐧𝐢𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐦 𝐏𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐜𝐲]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, August 5, 2025**
The Office of Student Welfare and Services (OSWS) released an advisory on July 31, 2025, regarding the implementation of a new uniform policy that formally recognizes the right of students to wear components of the existing prescribed uniform that align with their Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity, Gender Expression, and Sex Characteristics (SOGIESC).
This initiative aims to provide a space for members of the LGBTQIA+ community to express their authentic selves freely within the campus.
According to the advisory, the implementation of the proper wearing of uniforms, including this new provision, will start on September 8, 2025, for freshmen and transferees.
Meanwhile, for continuing students, the change will take effect on August 18, 2025. The wearing of the prescribed uniform will remain compulsory for all students from Monday to Thursday.
“In recognition of the spectrum of gender identities within our student body, the institution affirms the right of individuals to express their authentic selves through their attire,” the institution affirmed in its official statement.
Furthermore, on “wash days” (Friday, Saturday, Sunday, and Mid-Year Term), students have the right to choose their attire, as long as it adheres to the College’s prescribed school attire and maintains decency.
This means the students are now allowed to choose from the established guidelines for male and female clothing, specifically, the pants or skirts that are already part of the school’s approved uniform, to express their gender identity best, provided the chosen attire still adheres to the College’s overall dress code and decency standards.
The said office encourages the entire Gordon College community to ensure the implementation of this new policy.

# 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025
[Image: 10 HEIs sa Central Luzon, pasok sa Times Higher Education Impact Rankings 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, June 23, 2025**
Muling pinatunayan ng Central Luzon ang galing nito sa larangan ng edukasyon matapos mapasama ang 10 pampublikong Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) sa Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Ranking 2025.
Batay sa social media post ng Commission on Higher Education (CHED) Regional Office III, kabilang sa prestihiyosong listahan ang mga sumusunod na institusyon:
1. Central Luzon State University (801-1000)
2. Tarlac Agricultural University (1001-1500)
3. Bataan Peninsula State University (1501+)
4. Bulacan State University (1501+)
5. Don Honorio Ventura State University (1501+)
6. Nueva Ecija State University of Science and Technology (1501+)
7. Tarlac State University (1501+)
8. Gordon College (1501+)
9. Mabalacat City College (1501+)
10. City College of San Fernando, Pampanga (1501+)
Ang THE Impact Rankings ay sumusukat sa kontribusyon ng mga kolehiyo at unibersidad sa pag-abot ng United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), gaya ng dekalidad na edukasyon, gender equality, climate action, at iba pa.
Layunin nitong kilalanin at ipagdiwang ang mga institusyong aktibong nakikibahagi sa pagsusulong ng mas maayos at mas inklusibong mundo.
Ngayong taon, higit 2,000 universities mula sa 125 na bansa at teritoryo ang lumahok sa ranking. Sa bilang na ito, nasa 121 na institusyon sa Pilipinas ang kinilala, patunay ng lumlawak na global recognition pagdating sa kalidad ng edukasyon sa bansa.
Courtesy of CLTV36, click [here](https://cltv36.tv/10-heis-sa-central-luzon-pasok-sa-times-higher-education-impact-rankings-2025/?fbclid=IwY2xjawLhlMJleHRuA2FlbQIxMQABHjuiWKrpyze2X0ey_w9YqcxzFYqj-vVsA6VgMEYf5A0ksmU46n6TdywcL5ki_aem_Cu5pDIot_WbL-AJyV822ug) to read the full article.

# LIMC Packed with Students as New School Year Opens
[Image: LIMC Packed with Students as New School Year Opens]
[LIMC](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/limc/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 18, 2022**
Welcome Back to School at Gordon College is phenomenal as students spend their spare time inside the library reading books. Students from different departments utilize the library in doing their homework or completing their reading materials. This is highly unexpected because of the recent pandemic.
Since the recent pandemic, Gordon College has implemented a lot of new rules for students to follow in order for them to be safe and healthy. The same goes with the library since it is a place where students get to spend some time studying for their upcoming exams and projects. In fact, students are not allowed to bring food inside the library since not all food are safe to eat anymore because of lack of proper sanitation and hygiene practices during meal preparation.
Despite these new rules being implemented by Gordon College, many students still prefer staying inside the library because it gives them an opportunity to study without any distractions from other people or places around campus.

# Paulino welcomes 9k studes with new building; rates GC as highly university-ready
[Image: Paulino welcomes 9k studes with new building; rates GC as highly university-ready]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, October 18, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE MAIN CAMPUS — Coinciding with PAGLALAYAG 2025 (more commonly known as Apertura 2025), the newly acquired Gordon College Annex Building, located at Lower Kalaklan, Olongapo, was formally inaugurated on August 11.
In an interview with The Forefront, Olongapo City Mayor Atty. Rolen C. Paulino Jr. “wanted to be practical” in expanding Gordon College (GC), citing several important considerations.
Paulino believes that students applied to Gordon College for quality education, a main reason to attract 9,500 students this Academic Year (A.Y).
As the A.Y. 2025-2026 begins, the local chief executive highlighted their desire to make ongoing improvements to the current building and structure, ensuring new equipment is added as needed.
“We want to make sure that everything goes accordingly. Gusto talaga naming mag-expand,” the mayor said.
The back-to-back mayor of Olongapo City also considered improving and adding programs that would need more equipment and facilities.
“Tinitingnan din natin ‘yong possibility na mag-expand pa tayo kasi may clamor din to add additional [programs], whether more medical courses or engineering, lahat ‘yan pinag-iisipan namin,” the mayor added.
Now, the College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) has officially moved to the newly inaugurated annex building of the institution.
When asked about the institution’s readiness for university status, the Board of Trustees Chairperson gave the institution a rating of 8 to 9 out of 10, stressing that while “the qualifications [are] there and personnel [are] there”, there remains the challenge of “accumulating enough resources to qualify.”
Moreover, the chairperson spoke of the institution being already at that point as “the quality of education [that Gordon College] provides is at par with some of the universities.”
“Sa results ng [board] exams natin, we’re always competing with the best universities,” he added.
Furthermore, the mayor underscored the need to instill professionalism and discipline among students through the institution, aiming to make them employable and ready to become future managers. He also nurtured and wished for the students to apply values of professionalism and intelligence to work hand-in-hand when enrolled within the institution.
“[Please] know that the city government is here to support the institution and the students. The city is always proud of Gordon College. It’s the home of many top-notchers, many successful community leaders, both in business and society. Mataas ang accreditation natin, we are internationally ranked, [and] we are very proud of the school and very proud of its students,” he concluded. | via Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# Empowering Solo Parents: Gordon College Launches ‘Weave of Strength’ Initiative
[Image: Empowering Solo Parents: Gordon College Launches ‘Weave of Strength’ Initiative]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, April 8, 2026**
Empowering communities through sustainable livelihood and inclusive development, Weave of Strength: Turning Scraps into Sustainable Livelihood was conducted on April 7, 2026 at the Gordon College Function Hall. The initiative provided solo parents and economically disadvantaged individuals with practical skills in transforming recycled fabric scraps into sustainable sources of income.
The program featured an inspiring message from College President Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano and the support of Vice President for RDCES, Dr. Ediric D. Gadia, reinforcing the college’s commitment to community service and development.
With the support of the Rotary Club of Downtown Olongapo, and through the coordinated efforts of RDCES and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management as the implementing unit, the initiative reflects Gordon College’s core values of Character, Excellence, and Service.
To ensure long-term sustainability, the initiative adopts a capacity-building approach by equipping administrative personnel to serve as future facilitators, enabling continuous implementation and expanded community reach.

# CEAS Women’s Historic Smash Hit: Another Gold in Futsal Match
[Image: CEAS Women’s Historic Smash Hit: Another Gold in Futsal Match]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, September 19, 2025**
What a thrilling clash between pink and blue.
The College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors defeated College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks after a long showdown that ended in 3-0, and finished as champions in the Women’s Futsal match on the last day of SIKLAB 2025, held at New Asinan covered court.
The Mentors did not let their guard down ever since their first game, making sure everything is coordinated to one another until the final match.
Cheers roared through the court as the intensifying moments of the game went on, with Mentors maintaining their momentum throughout the match.
Despite the Sharks’ efforts to lessen the pressure, CEAS women clocked the game with a winning score of 3-0. | via Mariah Llave/The FOREFRONT

# Final presentation of Project METRO
[Image: Final presentation of Project METRO]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, April 17, 2026**
April 16, 2026 | Final presentation of Project METRO: Monitoring Enforcement Tracking Resource Online with DTI Zambales and Gordon College – College of Computer Studies.
Present during the final presentation are DTI3 CPD Acting Division Chief Jose Antonio Toledo, DTI Zambales CPD Division Chief Neil John M. Fabay, Gordon College CCS Dean Ronnie D. Luy, Gordon College CCS Asst Dean Armilyn T. Martinez, and Gordon College CCS Prof. Reynaldo G. Bautista. The panelist provided expert guidance and valuable insights to further refining the system.
Project METRO is intended to make market inspections and fair trade monitoring more efficient, transparent, and fully digitized. In practical terms, the system aims to help government personnel carry out inspections more systematically while improving documentation, reporting, and coordination in the field.
The use of a digital platform for inspections will also help strengthen public trust by making enforcement more transparent and easier to document. In many cases, digitized systems can support more consistent monitoring while reducing gaps in manual processes.

# Reading Literacy Training
[Image: Reading Literacy Training]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Saturday, June 10, 2023**
The GC CESU, Academic Affairs, and CEAS immersion instructors collaborated successfully to conduct a 3-day training for various CEAS academic programs. Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano played a pivotal role as the resource trainer, contributing to the training’s success. The training drew on the participation of a diverse group of student facilitators from ten programs within the College of Education, Arts, and Sciences. Specifically, 3rd-year students from BSEd (Mathematics, Science, English, Filipino, and Social Studies) and other programs like BPEd, BEEd, BCAEd, BECEd, and BAComm actively took part in the training. This training aligned with the goals of the MRP’s Reading Literacy Program for non-readers and out-of-school youth.

# Bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan, pinasinayaan sa Gordon College
[Image: Bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan, pinasinayaan sa Gordon College]
[CEAS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ceas/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 7, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE—Inukit na ang mga bagong lakan at lakambini ng kasaysayan kasabay ng panapos na selebrasyon ng Buwan ng Kasaysayan 2025 na may temang “Diwa ng Kasaysayan, Kabilin sa Kabataan” na idinaos noong Agosto 30, sa Gordon College Physical Education (PE) Hall sa pangunguna ng Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan (SMAP).
Nagwagi sina G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales mula sa BSEd Soc 3-A at Bb. Angel Mae Deseo mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A para sa Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan 2025.
Nasungkit naman nina G. Jhan Girbaud Hipolito mula sa BSEd Soc 2-A at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A ang korona bilang Ginoong Unang Gantimpala at Binibining Unang Gantimpala.
Namayagpag din bilang Ginoong Ikalawang Gantimpala at Binibining Ikalawang Gantimpala sina G. Rafael Ellado mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A at Bb. Gervy Sarmiento mula sa BSEd Soc 2-A.
Bukod sa Lakan at Lakambini 2025, iginawad din ang mga espesyal na parangal tulad ng Lunting Diwa at Best in Talent na parehong nakuha nina G. Jhan Girbaud Hipolito at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho.
Nakamit naman nina G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho ang mga titulong Best in Formal Attire at People’s Choice Award.
Samantala, inuwi naman nina Bb. Angel Mae Deseo at G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales ang karangalang Binibini at Ginoo ng Nakaraan.
“Nung sinabi na ako yung panalo, naging proud ako kasi nagbunga yung suporta ng pamilya at mga kaibigan, higit sa lahat, nagbunga yung effort at tiwala ko sa sarili” saad ng Lakan 2025.
“In-expect ko talaga na may chance akong manalo.” Ibinahagi din ni Lorenzen na pinaghirapan niya ang kanyang preparasyon, mula ensayo hanggang pagpapakilala at pagsagot sa tanong ng mga hurado.
Samantala, labis naman ang pasasalamat ni Bb. Angel Mae Deseo, Lakambini 2025, sa lahat ng tumulong sa kanya lalo na at noong panahong iyon ay masama pa ang kanyang karamdaman.
“Tinrangkaso po ako hanggang sa mismong laban, sa kabila nun ay ipinagpatuloy ko ang laban sapagkat nakikita ko ang aking mga kamag-aral (SOC 1-A) na nagtutulong-tulong upang matapos ang lahat ng aming kakailanganin,” saad ni Angel Mae Deseo.
“Lahat ng pagod at puyat ay naging makabuluhan sapagkat nasungkit namin ang korona at karangalan,” aniya. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# Colegio de Masinloc Visits Gordon College for Benchmarking Toward Institutional Recognition
[Image: Colegio de Masinloc Visits Gordon College for Benchmarking Toward Institutional Recognition]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, August 29, 2025**
On August 27, 2025, Gordon College had the privilege of hosting Colegio de Masinloc for a benchmarking visit as they gear up for their application for Institutional Recognition, a crucial step toward qualifying for the Free Higher Education program under the Universal Access to Quality Tertiary Education Act (Republic Act 10931).
Their delegation, headed by Dr. Feliciano M. Rosete Jr. – College President, explored Gordon College’s best practices and gathered insights on CHED’s requirements for Institutional Capacity, Program Offerings, and Physical Facilities. The visit also aligned with their plans to expand academic offerings with new programs in Computer Science, Entrepreneurship, Accountancy, and Civil Engineering.
Gordon College is honored to share its experiences and extend its support to partner institutions in advancing the quality and accessibility of higher education.

# Suspension of Transactions

ANNOUNCEMENT  
December 11, 2025
**Suspension of Transactions**
Please be informed that all College transactions will be suspended from Monday, December 15, to Tuesday, December 16, 2025, due to the Strategic Planning for all College Personnel.
Additionally, transactions at the College Registrar’s Office will be suspended earlier, on Friday, December 12, 2025, for their attendance to Assembly of Registrars in Region 3.
All transactions will resume on Wednesday, December 17, 2025.
